coredata.dc:description: In this work, silicon dots (SiDs) were synthesized and surface-engineered by introducing Gum Arabic (GA), which were utilized as novel non-metallic quantum dots corrosion inhibitors for Q235 carbon steel in HCl solution. A comparative analysis was conducted on the synthesis, characterization, and inhibition performance, confirming the surface engineering can enhance their anticorrosion ability while maintaining the excitation and emission properties. Weight loss tests confirmed a corrosion rate decrease from 8.38 g·m–2·h–1 (blank) to 0.53 g·m–2·h–1 (100 mg/L GA-SiDs), achieving 93.68 % inhibition efficiency. Electrochemical tests revealed a polarization resistance of 1013 Ω cm2 for GA-SiDs, significantly higher than the blank (54.88 Ω cm2), while polarization curves confirmed mixed-type inhibition with a corrosion current density reduction to 1.48 × 10–4 A·cm–2. Thermodynamic and surface/interface analysis demonstrated the inhibition mechanism: physical adsorption interaction, chemisorption coordination, and hydrophobic film formation. This work provides a sustainable strategy for designing non-metallic quantum dot inhibitors, combining high performance, cost-effectiveness, and environmental compatibility.

head.ce:title: Surface engineering of silicon dots with Gum Arabic for enhanced corrosion inhibition of Q235 steel in acidic environments

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, silicon dots (SiDs) were synthesized and surface-engineered by introducing Gum Arabic (GA), which were utilized as novel non-metallic quantum dots corrosion inhibitors for Q235 carbon steel in HCl solution. A comparative analysis was conducted on the synthesis, characterization, and inhibition performance, confirming the surface engineering can enhance their anticorrosion ability while maintaining the excitation and emission properties. Weight loss tests confirmed a corrosion rate decrease from 8.38 g·m–2·h–1 (blank) to 0.53 g·m–2·h–1 (100 mg/L GA-SiDs), achieving 93.68 % inhibition efficiency. Electrochemical tests revealed a polarization resistance of 1013 Ω cm2 for GA-SiDs, significantly higher than the blank (54.88 Ω cm2), while polarization curves confirmed mixed-type inhibition with a corrosion current density reduction to 1.48 × 10–4 A·cm–2. Thermodynamic and surface/interface analysis demonstrated the inhibition mechanism: physical adsorption interaction, chemisorption coordination, and hydrophobic film formation. This work provides a sustainable strategy for designing non-metallic quantum dot inhibitors, combining high performance, cost-effectiveness, and environmental compatibility.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: During the operation of the circulating cooling water system, deposits (dirt) often accumulate on the metal surfaces of its cooling equipment [1]. Most cooling equipment, such as heat exchangers, are made of Q235 carbon steel [2]. The presence of deposits significantly reduces the cooling effect of the heat exchanger and creates favorable conditions for corrosion under scale [3]. Typically, hydrochloric acid is utilized for chemical cleaning of the cooling water system and undergoes two types of reactions: one being a dissolution reaction of deposits, another type of corrosion reaction occurs in the metal beneath the sediment when exposed to a hydrochloric acid cleaning solution. During this process, the anodic reaction involves the oxidation of carbon steel, resulting in the generation of ferrous ions that enter the cleaning solution and leave two electrons on the surface of the carbon steel's metal matrix. Simultaneously, a significant number of H+ within the HCl cleaning solution accept these two electrons on the surface of carbon steel's metal matrix and are reduced to H2, which is then released from the surface of carbon steel in bubble form. Hence, it is imperative to incorporate appropriate corrosion inhibitors into the cleaning solution to elevate the overpotential for hydrogen evolution or iron dissolution, thereby mitigating the corrosion rate of the metal matrix. Research on traditional corrosion inhibitors has established a relatively comprehensive theoretical framework encompassing designing synthesis, experimental exploration, theoretical calculation and mechanism analysis [4]. However, when considering practical application, three primary limitations must be taken into account: (1) high toxicity; (2) expensive cost; (3) poor water solubility; (4) limited application scope.

Quantum dots, nanoscale semiconductor particles with tunable optoelectronic properties, have revolutionized diverse fields ranging from biomedical imaging and energy storage to environmental remediation [5,6]. Over the last five years, non-metallic quantum dots have been extensively employed in the realm of metal corrosion and protection owing to their exceptional biocompatibility and photochemical characteristics [7]. In 2024, the Scopus citation database contains over 200 pertinent reports. Recent research has predominantly focused on the utilization of carbon quantum dots (CDs) as corrosion inhibitors [8,9]. CDs achieve inhibition primarily through two mechanisms: (i) physical adsorption via electrostatic interactions between charged functional groups (–COOH, –NH2) and the metal surface, and (ii) chemisorption through coordination bonds with transition metals (e.g., Fe–N/S/O bonds) [10]. Despite their success, CDs corrosion inhibitors face critical limitations: First, the majority of reported CDs achieve corrosion resistance through the doping of N or S elements on their surfaces. Second, most studies focus on post- surface modification performance without systematically comparing it to the intrinsic properties of unmodified QDs [11].

Silicon quantum dots (SiDs) represent a groundbreaking advancement in non-metallic corrosion inhibitors due to their unique physicochemical properties, which distinguish them from conventional inhibitors and other quantum dots (e.g., carbon dots): First, abundance and sustainability. Silicon is the second most abundant element in the Earth's crust, guaranteeing low-cost and scalable production of SiDs in contrast to inhibitors based on rare elements [12]. Unlike carbon dots derived from complex precursors, SiDs can be synthesized from silica-rich industrial byproducts, conforming to the principles of the circular economy [13]. Second, biocompatibility and environmental safety. SiDs demonstrate negligible toxicity and biodegradability, addressing the ecological concerns related to toxic heavy metal-based inhibitors [14]. Third, inherent corrosion inhibition. Silicon demonstrates outstanding performance in corrosion inhibition, with traditional inorganic silicon-based inhibitors primarily consisting of anodic type silicates, which can be utilized as low-phosphorus inhibitor formulations while organic silicon-based inhibitors, such as organosilanes, organosiloxanes, and materials modified with organosilane coupling agents, exhibit outstanding hydrophobic and high-temperature resistance properties [15]. These characteristics make silicon well-suited for corrosion inhibition and rust prevention in high-temperature environments. However, there are also some urgent problems to be solved for SiDs. Due to the hydrophobic functional groups (such as Si–H bonds) on their surface coverage, they usually have poor water solubility and it is difficult to control their size, and application research is relatively lagging behind [16].

Recently, there has been rapid advancement in the research of utilizing biomacromolecules and colloidal materials for the stabilization of nanoparticles [17,18]. Gum Arabic (GA), derived from Acacia trees and composed of glycoproteins, arabinose and galactose, is found to contain abundant active functional groups (–OH and –COOH), demonstrates distinctive comprehensive advantages [19]: First, molecular structure and functional adjustability. Verma et al. revealed that its abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups bestow it with outstanding metal chelation ability, and it can form a stable adsorption layer with the metal substrate via coordination bonds [20]. Additionally, its branched chain structure can further regulate the interface performance through chemical modification (such as esterification and graft copolymerization) [21]. Second, interface engineering potential. The unique amphiphilic nature of Arabic gum enables it to effectively stabilize nanoparticles and enhance the interface binding with nanoparticles through hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces [22,23]. For instance, Song discovered that the multi-branch structure of GA-SeNPs fabricated using GA successfully enhanced the thermal stability of nanoparticles [24]. In our previous work, we successfully synthesized water-soluble sulfur quantum dots (SQDs) inhibitors using GA and carboxymethylcellulose as the stabilizer [25,26]. During the formation of SQDs, GA is adsorbed onto the particle surfaces. The GA macromolecule serve as physical cross-links between SQDs, thereby enhancing their water dispersibility. Inspired by these, we hypothesize that the employment of GA as a stabilizing agent in the production of SiDs will similarly improve their water solubility and stability.

In this work, pure SiDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal method while GA was additionally introduced to obtain the enhanced water solubility and corrosion inhibition performance of silicon quantum dots (GA-SiDs). A comparative analysis was conducted on the synthesis, characterization, and corrosion inhibition performance of two types of silicon quantum dots. The dynamic adsorption process and evolution law of silicon quantum dots on metal surfaces/interfaces were systematically examined in order to elucidate the intrinsic relationship between the active structure and their corrosion inhibition performance. Combined with the silicon's inherent characteristics, such as excellent biocompatibility, environmental friendliness, and low cost, this work is significantly valuable for the development and performance prediction of novel non-metallic quantum dots corrosion inhibitors in both theoretical and practical aspects.

Gum Arabic (GA, 98 %), 1-trimethylsilyllimidazole (TSIM, 98 %), and sodium citrate (98 %) were procured from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Ltd. (China). Q235 carbon steels were procured from Jiangsu Gaoyou Motian Ltd. (China) and have the following composition (wt%): carbon, 0.17; manganese, 0.37; silicon, 0.20; sulfur, 0.03; phosphorus, 0.01 with the remaining being iron. Prior to testing, the specimens underwent mechanical polishing with silicon carbide paper of varying grit sizes (300, 1000, and 1500) followed by degreasing with acetone and vacuum drying. Deionized water was utilized for preparing all aqueous solutions.

The synthesis process for silicon quantum dots is illustrated in Fig. 1. Firstly, 1.8 g of GA and 1.8 g of sodium citrate were dissolved into 48 mL of ultra-pure water. Subsequently, 12 mL of TSIM was slowly added into the mixed solution, which was then transferred to a high-pressure reactor for hydrothermal reaction at 220°C for 2 h. Following this, the product underwent centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min, and the resulting supernatant was subsequently purified by dialysis (M w: 1000 Da) against deionized water for a duration of 24 h to remove solid impurities. After the drying process, the GA-SiDs were acquired using a freeze dryer at –50°C and under a pressure of 10 Pa. The SiDs were synthesized using consistent procedures, excluding the incorporation of GA. The yields of SiDs and GA-SiDs were calculated to be 68 % and 74 %, respectively, based on the mass of the final product relative to the initial precursors. This enhancement was ascribed to the dual stabilization effect of GA, which inhibits oxidation and aggregation during the solvothermal process.

The morphology of silicon quantum dots was examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai F20, FEI) and high-resolution TEM. The structure and surface chemical groups were verified through a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, VERTEX 70, Bruker). The optical properties were measured using a transient/steady state fluorescence spectrophotometer (FLS920, EDINBURGH). The surface charge and size distribution were analyzed using Nanoparticle size and Zeta potential analyzer (ZEN3690, Malvern). The crystal status and elemental composition were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250 XI, Thermo Fisher Scientific).

The Q235 carbon steels were submerged in a 0.5 M HCl solution (500 mL) for 24 h at 25°C, both in the absence and presence of silicon quantum dots. Prior to the experiments, the samples were meticulously weighed. After immersion, the specimens underwent a cleaning process with deionized water and ethanol. Subsequent to the application of a drying process, which was performed under vacuum at 40°C for 15 min to prevent oxidation, adhering to ASTM G1–03 guidelines, the samples were re-weighed in order to determine both the corrosion rate (ν) and inhibition efficiency (η wl):

where W represents average weight difference (g), S denotes total exposed surface area (m2), t stands for the duration of immersion (h), ν
0 and ν represent mean corrosion rates of carbon steels in the absence and presence of silicon quantum dots (g·m–2·h–1). The analytical balance (Precision ± 0.1 mg) was calibrated using NIST-traceable weights. Triplicate measurements were followed to minimize experimental variability, with standard deviations ≤ 1.0 % for inhibition efficiencies.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were conducted using a Gamry Reference 3000 workstation to assess inhibition performance of silicon quantum dots. A three-electrode cell configuration was utilized, with a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, a platinum mesh counter electrode with an exposed surface area of 8.0 cm2, and a Q235 steel sample (4.00 cm2 working area) as the working electrode. The larger surface area of the Pt counter electrode ensures that current density limitations from the counter electrode are negligible during measurements. EIS tests were performed with an amplitude of 5 mV, scanning frequencies from 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz. Potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) curves were performed after EIS tests with a scan rate of 1 mV/s from – 750 to – 150 mV. The Gamry Reference 3000 potentiostat was validated with a dummy cell (100 Ω || 1 µF), confirming impedance accuracy within ± 2.0 %. The measurements were conducted at a controlled temperature of 25°C ± 1°C and were replicated three times to ensure the robustness and reliability of the results.

After the electrochemical tests, the surface morphology and elemental distribution of these samples were examined by Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Sigma 300, ZEISS) and energy dispersive X-ray (EDS) spectroscopy. The water contact angle (WCA, DSA25S) test was performed at a temperature of 25°C ± 1°C and relative humidity levels of 30 % to study the surface hydrophobic/hydrophilic property. The three-dimensional micrographs, height distribution, roughness, and phase diagram were examined using a three-dimensional laser microscope (3D-LSM, Keyence VK-X150) and atomic force microscope (AFM, Dimension Icon, Bruker). The surface chemical composition of Q235 steel was analyzed using Time-of-Flight Secondary Ion Mass Spectrometry (TOF-SIMS, PHI nanoTOF II). The composition of the corrosion products formed on the steel surface was confirmed through XPS testing.

The TEM images ( Fig. 2) show the morphologies of as-synthesized silicon quantum dots. The average diameter of bare SiDs is 3.65 nm, while that of GA-SiDs decreases to 3.56 nm, as calculated from measurements of 110 particles (inset of Fig. 2a,b). The highly resolved (2 2 0) lattice planes with a spacing of ∼ 0.19 nm in the High-Resolution TEM images (inset of Fig. 2a,b) indicate the exceptional crystallinity of the silicon quantum dots [27]. The FTIR spectroscopy of SiDs is presented in Fig. 2c, revealing the following results: FTIR (KBr), ν: 3435, 1617, 1395, 1007, and 774 cm–1. The Si−O−Si stretching peak at 1007 cm–1 confirms the successful preparation of SiDs [28]. The characteristic peak at 3435 cm–1 is attributed to the –OH and –NH2 groups on the surface of SiDs [29]. Additionally, the peak at 774 and 1617 cm–1 corresponds to the wagging and bending vibration of N–H groups, while the peak at 1395 cm–1 is attributed to C–N stretching vibrations [30]. Upon the introduction of GA, new peaks are discernible at 1385, 1421, and 2925 cm–1 in the FTIR spectroscopy of GA-SiDs. The distinctive vibrations of glucuronic acids from the structure of GA are represented by the peaks at 1421 and 1385 cm–1, corresponding to symmetric stretching of CO and bending of –OH, respectively [31]. Additionally, the peak observed at 2925 cm–1 provides confirmation of the existence of R–CH2–R groups originating from the structure of GA [32]. These newly observed peaks provide evidence for surface modification of SiDs due to the introduction of GA. In addition, the absorption peaks observed at 1145 and 1070 cm–1 indicate the presence of C–O–C/Si–O–Si bonds, while the peak detected at 912 cm–1 signifies the existence of Si–O bonds [33]. These findings suggest an abundance of hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of the GA-SiDs, which significantly enhance their water solubility and stability due to the presence of these functional hydrophilic groups.

The photophysical behavior and optical activities are considered the most prominent characteristics of quantum-sized materials. The utilization of fluorescence properties for visualizing corrosion inhibitors on engineering equipment surfaces represents a promising new area of research. The confirmation of silicon quantum dots photoluminescence is achieved through fluorophotometer analysis ( Fig. 3a,b). Both the SiDs and GA-SiDs products exhibit a light-yellow appearance in daylight and emit blue fluorescence with a wavelength of 424 nm when excited at 360 nm. The surface charge measurements (Fig. 3c,d) are conducted to further validate the dispersion of 1000 mg/L silicon quantum dots in deionized water. The average size of the GA-SiDs is approximately 13.7 nm, exhibiting a significant reduction from the size of the bare SiDs (112.0 nm), which is attributed to the bare SiDs exhibit a spherical shape and tend to agglomerate in their solid dry state due to surface tension; however, the surface hydroxyl and carboxyl functionalization of GA-SiDs results in a notable enhancement in water solubility, thereby retarding the agglomeration of silicon nanoparticles in deionized water. The surface charge carried by silicon quantum dots is examined through zeta-potential measurements (Fig. 3e,f), yielding an average value of − 15.1 mV for SiDs and − 11.4 mV for GA-SiDs.

The surface composition of the silicon dots is determined through XPS analysis. The XPS survey spectrum ( Fig. 4a) of SiDs and GA-SiDs reveals four prominent peaks on both spectra, with major peaks at 101.8, 284.8, 399.0, and 531.1 eV corresponding to Si 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. Corresponding atomic percentages are calculated based on peak area (Fig. 4b), demonstrating a significant increase in oxygen atomic percentages of GA-SiDs, which results from the –OH and –COOH surface functionalization. In the case of SiDs, the peak observed at 101.6 eV in the high-resolution Si 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 4c) unequivocally confirms the presence of Si−O in the chemical environment, while the high-resolution O 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 4d) provides additional evidence for the existence of oxygen as an O−Si at 531.3 eV [34]. The N 1 s high-resolution spectra (Fig. 4e) reveals a distinct N − Si peak at 398.3 eV, and the high-resolution C 1 s spectra (Fig. 4f) demonstrates the presence of carbon in three distinct chemical environments, including the C−Si (283.7 eV), C−N (284.9 eV), and C−OH/C−O−C (286.0 eV) functional groups, respectively [35]. The comprehensive XPS results confirm the diverse surface states of the SiDs. These assignments above are in accordance with the observed binding energies.

Fig. 5a,c illustrate the variation of ν for Q235 steel and η wl of silicon quantum dots, and the results are presented in Table S1. For comparison, GA at a concentration of 100 mg/L shows an ηwl of 72.17 %, which is significantly lower than that of GA-SiDs (93.68 %) under the same conditions. The higher efficiency of GA-SiDs compared with GA alone is in line with previous studies [36], and it is attributed to the fact that GA-modified silicon quantum dots demonstrate superior inhibition due to the combination of hydrophobic barrier formation and coordination interactions. In addition, it is evident the presence of higher concentrations of silicon quantum dots leads to a decrease in corrosion rate and an increase in inhibition efficiency. Notably, at 100 mg/L, the maximum η wl of SiDs and GA-SiDs reach 92.96 and 93.68 %, respectively, with the corresponding minimum ν for Q235 steel of 0.59 and 0.53 g·m–2·h–1, respectively. The superior inhibition efficiency of GA-SiDs compared to SiDs arises from the synergistic effects enabled by GA modification. First, GA’s hydrophilic functional groups (–OH and –COOH) significantly enhance the water solubility of SiDs, reducing their agglomerated particle size, which ensures uniform dispersion and adsorption on the Q235 steel surface. Second, GA introduces additional adsorption sites through its –OH and –COOH groups, enabling stronger chemisorption. Third, GA’s branched polysaccharide structure facilitates cross-linking between SiDs, forming a dense hydrophobic protective film that acts as a physical barrier against Cl– and H+ diffusion. In contrast, SiDs lack sufficient functional groups for effective chemisorption and exhibit poor dispersibility, leading to uneven surface coverage and inferior protection. This multifunctional role of GA: enhancing solubility, enabling chemisorption, and reinforcing physical barriers, collectively underpins the exceptional corrosion inhibition performance of GA-SiDs.

The adsorption behavior of SiDs and GA-SiDs is analyzed using Langmuir, Freundlich, and Temkin isotherms (Table S2). The Langmuir model exhibits superior linearity (R 2 = 0.9987, 0.9989) and slopes near unity (1.057, 1.051), consistent with monolayer adsorption. In contrast, Freundlich (R 2 = 0.9743, 0.9821) and Temkin models (R 2 = 0.9562, 0.9638) show weaker fits, ruling out multilayer or heterogeneous adsorption. Thus, the results of weight loss tests are fitted with the chosen Langmuir adsorption isotherm, as evidenced by the equation:

where K ads is the equilibrium constant, with C representing inhibitor concentration and θ representing surface coverage.

As depicted in Fig. 5b,d, the high linear correlation coefficient (R 2 = 0.9987, 0.9989) and steep slope of the curve, approaching 1 (1.057, 1.051), provide compelling evidence that the model effectively captures the adsorption behavior of SiDs and GA-SiDs, respectively. The Langmuir isotherm suggest a single layer on the surface and each adsorbent occupy one active center, and the model slopes slightly exceeding 1.0 suggest each adsorbed molecule occupies 1.05 active sites on the metal surface [37]. The adsorption behavior of silicon quantum dots is further elucidated through analysis of the adsorption-free energy (

where R represents the molar gas constant (8.314 J·mol–1·K–1) while T denotes the temperature (298 K). The values of

for SiDs and GA-SiDs are calculated to be approximate –31.398 kJ·mol–1 and –31.825 kJ·mol–1, respectively. While the values indicate slightly stronger chemisorption than typical physisorption (> −20 kJ·mol–1), they remain less negative than pure covalent bonding (< −40 kJ·mol–1), confirming a mixed adsorption mechanism involving both physisorption and chemisorption [39]. First, physisorption: Initial adsorption occurs via electrostatic attraction between charged inhibitors molecules and the metal surface. Second, chemisorption: Subsequent coordination bonds form between Fe atoms and functional groups on inhibitors. The above adsorption process promotes the development of a protective layer, which effectively shields the iron surface from the HCl solution [40].

The EIS technique relies on the instantaneous response of the electrode/solution interface to an equivalent circuit, which is capable of analyzing alternating-current (AC) impedance data based on a corrosion process model using electronic circuit methods, thereby characterizing the electrode's corrosion behavior and determining parameters such as polarization resistance (R p), corrosion rate, and electrochemical mechanism. Fig. 6 presents the Nyquist plots illustrating the measurements of electrodes at different immersion times, and the complete Nyquist figure under varying concentrations of silicon quantum dots are illustrated in Figure S1. It is evident when the working electrodes soak in a blank solution, the capacitance arc diameter decreases over time (Figure S1a). Upon the addition of SiDs (Figure S1b-f), there is an increase in the diameter of the capacitive loop, which gradually enlarges with higher concentrations. Above all, the addition of GA-SiDs (Figure S1g-k) results in a notably larger capacitance arc diameter, indicating enhanced corrosion inhibition performance compared to the SiDs-containing condition. The Bode plots in Figure S2 illustrate the presence of distinct time constants in the blank condition at low frequencies while the high-frequency time constant is associated with double-layer capacitance. In general, the maximum phase angles increase with higher inhibitor concentration [41]. The phase angle curves in Figure S2 also indicate an increase in maximum value and a broader signal with the prolonged immersion time, suggesting a denser inhibitor layer with reduced pore density [42]. The compact SiDs and GA-SiDs film gradually impedes the transfer of caustic matter due to its diminutive void dimension. Notably, the continuous decrease of phase angles in the blank solution and gradual increase in the solution with SiDs and GA-SiDs suggest that most active sites on the Q235 surface are blocked.

To determine the R p of Q235 steel which is inversely proportional to corrosion rate, the equivalent circuits (Fig. 6d) are selected to model the electrochemical interface of Q235 steel in 0.5 M HCl with and without silicon quantum dots. The fitted parameters are shown in Table 1 and the full datasets are listed in Table S3. Where, R s represent the electrolyte solution resistance and this value remains nearly constant across experiments, as the solution conductivity does not vary significantly. R f represents the resistance of the adsorbed inhibitor layer (SiDs/GA-SiDs) on the steel surface while a higher R f indicates a denser, more protective film [43]. R ct is charge transfer resistance of the iron oxide layer for the Faradaic corrosion reactions. CPE dl models the double-layer capacitance at the metal/solution interface while surface roughness and inhibitor adsorption typically lead to non-ideal capacitive behavior. CPE f accounts for the non-ideal capacitive behavior of the inhibitor film. The use of CPE (instead of an ideal capacitor) is necessary due to surface heterogeneity caused by inhibitor adsorption. n represents the dispersion coefficient, which signifies the phase shift and Y 0 is the amplitude of the CPE. χ
2 means the fitting error of the parameters. The inhibition efficiency (η EIS) is assessed by calculating the R p, which is calculated by the following equations:

where R 0 p is the initial polarization resistance in a blank solution. η EIS and R p versus immersion time in 0.5 M HCl solution containing different concentrations of SiDs and GA-SiDs are illustrated in Fig. 7a-d. The observed low R 0 p indicates severe corrosion of Q235 steel. The introduction of SiDs demonstrates a sustained inhibitory effect and even after 24 h of immersion, R p remains higher than that under the blank condition. However, it is evident the R p values of SiDs are significantly lower than those of GA-SiDs, and the η EIS values after 24 h of immersion for GA-SiDs are calculated at 95.57, 96.02, 97.05, 97.30, and 97.50 % for the concentrations of 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 mg/L, respectively. Prolonged immersion in acidic media results in uneven and less stable formation of the inhibitor's cover layer due to metal surface dissolution, ultimately promoting inhibitor desorption once equilibrium is reached between dissolution and adsorption rates, as indicated in Fig. 7b by an initial increase in R p (approximately 1–6 h) attributed to adhesion and coverage of GA-SiDs, followed by a notable decrease in R p during subsequent stages (approximately 6–24 h) due to desorption. For example, when the working electrode is submerged in 100 mg/L GA-SiDs solution, the R p value varies from 1013 Ω cm2 (1 h) to 1466 Ω cm2 (12 h), indicating an ongoing adsorption of GA-SiDs onto the iron surface. Subsequently, following an extended immersion period of 12–24 h, the R p diminishes to 975.0 Ω cm2, which remains significantly higher than R 0 p(a continuous decline from 54.88 to 24.38 Ω cm2), exhibiting the extended-term effectiveness of GA-SiDs in inhibiting corrosion. As a result, the inclusion of GA-SiDs significantly enhances R p compared to the sample with SiDs, due to the surface hydroxyl and carboxylation which greatly improve the water solubility and adsorption sites of silicon dots, thereby enhancing their corrosion inhibition properties. Additionally, the adsorption of silicon nanoparticles at the metal/solution interface leads to reduced corrosion reactivity.

The kinetics of electrochemical reactions serve as the foundation for determining the corrosion rate of metals exposed to corrosive media (electrolytes). The kinetics of reactions taking place on electrode surfaces are contingent upon the electrode potential. Consequently, the reaction rate is predominantly reliant on the flow rate of electrons through the metal-electrolyte interface. If an electrochemical system is in a state of equilibrium, the overall reaction rate will be zero. In contrast, chemical kinetics impact reaction rates, while electrochemical kinetics primarily influence corrosion rates. The electrochemical kinetics of corroding metals can be characterized by the following polarization parameters: corrosion potential (E corr), corrosion current density (i corr), anodic and cathodic Tafel slope (b a, b c) [44]. Therefore, polarization curves (E corr vs. lg i corr) can provide insights into the corrosion behavior of silicon quantum dots (Fig. 7e,f) and the corresponding parameters are summarized in Table 2. The corrosion inhibition efficiency (η PDP) is determined using the following equation:

It is evident that in the absence of the inhibitors, the i corr is denoted as 2.35 × 10–3 A·cm–2 and the E corr as – 0.471 V. upon introduction of the SiDs, both anodic and cathodic polarization curves shift towards the low-current density region. For instance, i corr decreases to 1.97 × 10–4 A·cm–2 and the E corr decreases to – 0.491 V after adding 100 mg/L of SiDs. In the case of GA-SiDs, there is a more pronounced decline in i corr to 1.48 × 10–4 A·cm–2. Furthermore, E corr shift towards cathodic domains slightly (about 20 mV for 100 mg/L). Therefore, it should be concluded that SiDs function as mixed-type corrosion inhibitor with domination of cathodic action [40]. It is apparent b a of SiDs increase from 0.1073 V dec–1 (blank) to 0.1769 V dec–1 (100 mg/L), indicating a probable alteration in anodic dissolution kinetics [37,45]. This rise likely stems from the formation of a partially passivating SiDs layer, which hinders Fe oxidation by increasing the activation energy for anodic charge transfer [46]. In addition, b a of GA-SiDs reduce at higher concentrations (e.g., 60–100 mg/L), suggesting a transition to diffusion-controlled inhibition as the hydrophobic film thickens [47]. The value b c of both inhibitors significantly reduce (e.g., bc of GA-SiDs varies from 0.1039 to 0.0362). This 67 % reduction implies a possible change in the mechanism of cathodic reaction, as adsorbed GA-SiDs block active sites and restrict H+ access. The η PDP values of SiDs range from 78.43 % at 20 mg/L to 91.62 % at 100 mg/L, while that of GA-SiDs range from 88.26 % at 20 mg/L to 93.70 % at 100 mg/L, which are close to the weight loss results. It can therefore be inferred that a stable adsorption film induced by SiDs and GA-SiDs forms on the iron surface, effectively shielding it against corrosive attack and providing significant inhibitive ability for iron corrosion in a 0.5 M HCl medium. In conclusion, the PDP test has demonstrated that SiDs and GA-SiDs suppress the electrode reaction, leading to a reduction in the corrosion rate and a consequent alteration of the polarization curve trend for the inhibited electrode reaction.

In order to examine the microstructure and investigate the surface hydrophobic/hydrophilic characteristics of Q235 steel, we conduct SEM/EDS and WCA tests for visual analysis. As illustrated in Figure 8a1,a2, the polished steel presents a smooth surface, albeit with some minor initial scratches evident upon magnification times. The corresponding WCA (Figure 8a3) measures 84.2°, indicating a hydrophilic nature of the freshly treated Q235 steel. Consequently, it is susceptible to erosion in acidic environments. Upon immersion of the freshly treated Q235 steel in an HCl solution for 24 h, corrosion is found to occur throughout the entire surface area, suggesting strong susceptibility to HCl-induced corrosion. Furthermore, Figure 8b1,b2 depicts the microstructure of Q235 steel, characterized by a rough surface and significant corrosion-induced cracking. The WCA sharply decreases to 25.5° in Figure 8b3, which is attributed to the highly corroded surface. However, the introduction of silicon quantum dots such as SiDs ( Fig. 8c) leads to the disappearance of a significant portion of these characteristics, particularly in the case of GA-SiDs (Fig. 8d), resulting in a more uniform surface on the Q235 steel with only minimal signs of corrosion visible. This suggests that silicon quantum dots exhibit a notable inhibitory impact on iron, and GA-SiDs demonstrate superior performance in corrosion inhibition. Additionally, the WCA rapidly increases to 80.2° and 81.3° upon introduction of SiDs and GA-SiDs, respectively, approaching that of the polished state as demonstrated by Figure 8c3 and Figure 8d3, which may be attributed to the hydrophobic effect of a significant quantity of silicon elements within the structure of SiDs and GA-SiDs, which evenly adsorb onto the surface of the iron. The EDS analysis (Fig. 8e) is conducted to assess the surface composition of Q235 steel specimens immersed in a 0.5 M HCl solution containing 100 mg/L GA-SiDs. The presence of Si element is observed, along with uniform dispersion of C, N, O, and Fe elements on the iron substrate. Above all, the corrosion inhibition effect achieved by silicon quantum dots can be attributed to their ability to adsorb onto the Q235 steel surface and thereby block the erosion caused by the HCl solution.

3D-LSM and AFM are capable of providing in-depth observation of 3D micrographs of metallic surfaces, particularly those that are corroded, with high sensitivity. They also allow for the acquisition of a series of surface roughness parameters. Fig. 9 presents the 3D-LSM and AFM images depicting the Q235 steel surfaces in various states. The polished iron surface (Figure 9a1,a2) appears smooth, flat, and uniform in the 3D-LSM images. However, slight imperfections are observed in the AFM images (Figure 9a3), which may be attributed to inherent defects within the iron itself. Additionally, the substrate is yellow as shown in Figure 10a4. Upon immersion in a solution without inhibitor (Fig. 9b), extensive corrosion is evident on the entire iron surface, resulting in extreme roughness. Notably, numerous pits and cracks are distributed across the entire iron surface. The 3D-LSM image (Figure 9b1) reveals the presence of peaks on the surface, as evidenced by the height distribution diagram in Figure 9b2, indicating that corrosion products are distributed across almost the entire iron surface. This observation is further supported by the significant fluctuation depicted in Figure 9b3 and the presence of uniformly covered deep-colored particles in Figure 9b4. On the contrary, upon addition of silicon quantum dots to 0.5 M HCl solution, corrosion on the iron surface is nearly eliminated, confirming the exceptional inhibitive performance of SiDs and GA-SiDs as demonstrated in Fig. 9c,d. However, the difference between the protected iron surfaces with SiDs and GA-SiDs in 3D-LSM and AFM images is not easily distinguishable. Additionally, the corresponding surface parameters are presented in Table S4, encompassing arithmetic mean height, peak curvature (S a, S pc), maximum height (S z), texture aspect, interface extended area ratio (S tr, S dr), mean square, average surface roughness (R q, R a), and peak to valley (P-V). It is evident that all the parameters for the Q235 steel sample corroded by HCl are significantly higher than those of the uncorroded sample and the protected sample by SiDs and GA-SiDs. Notably, all of the parameters of GA-SiDs are considerably lower than those of SiDs, indicating that GA-SiDs with superior performance are uniformly adsorbed on iron and result in a smoother surface on the protected iron. In conclusion, the 3D-LSM and AFM findings are in line with the SEM and WCA findings, indicating that the iron surfaces exposed to a GA-SiDs-containing solution exhibit a relatively smooth texture and significantly reduced corrosion on Q235 steel. Accordingly, the GA-SiDs with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups exhibit enhanced corrosion inhibition performance.

TOF-SIMS demonstrates significantly enhanced sensitivity in the detection of surface substances, enabling qualitative detection of monolayer substances on the sample surface. The surface elements of Q235 steel immersed for 24 h in an anticorrosive medium are further analyzed using TOF-SIMS, and the resulting mass spectra are depicted in Fig. 10. Fig. 10a,b illustrate the mass spectra obtained in negative ions mode (m/z 0–100). The results reveal binding bonds between Fe atoms on the Q235 steel surface and C, N, and O. Signals from heteroatoms such as O–, CN–, and C2HO– are detected in the spectrum as a result of the adsorption of active components such as nitrogen and oxygen atoms on the surface of Q235 steel [48]. In the positive ions mode (m/z 0–100) in Fig. 10c–f, in addition to the coordination of Fe with C and O, there is also evidence of coordination between Fe and N. The spectrum labeling encompasses hydrocarbon derivatives, such as CH–, C2H–, C4H–, C4H3 +, C6H5 +, C6H9 +, and C7H7 +. The generation of these fragment ions may be attributed to the ionization of impurities during the detection process or the fragmentation of silicon dots. The detection of iron compounds including FeOH–, FeO2 –, FeO2H–, and Fe+ suggests the attachment of silicon dots onto the surface of Q235 steel [49]. According to the frontier orbital theory, heteroatoms such as N and O act as electron donors by providing electrons to the vacant d orbitals of Fe in order to form coordination bonds, while simultaneously accepting electrons from these orbitals to establish back-donation bonds. This mechanism enables the binding between silicon dots and Q235 steel, providing an explanation for the ions produced from the breakdown of different iron oxides mentioned previously.

Fig. 11 illustrates the XPS analysis of corrosion products formed on a Q235 steel surface in a 0.5 M HCl solution containing 100 mg/L GA-SiDs after 24 h of immersion. The full-range XPS spectrum indicates the corrosion products found to contain the five elements including C, N, Fe, O, Si, and Cl, respectively. The presence of Si element indicates successful adsorption of GA-SiDs on the metal surface. The C 1 s spectrum shows three distinct peaks corresponding to C–C (284.8 eV), C–O (285.2 eV), and C–N (284.3 eV) [50]. The N 1 s spectrum is characterized by the presence of two distinct peaks corresponding to N–Fe (398.8 eV) and N amine (399.9 eV) [51]. The Fe 2p3/2 spectrum may show two peaks, which could be attributed to zero-valence Fe (706.8 eV), Fe(II) (710.5 eV), Fe–N (711.0 eV), and Fe2O3 (712.7 eV), indicating coordination between nitrogen species and iron atoms [52]. The O 1 s spectrum exhibits three distinct peaks, which can be ascribed to O–C (529.9 eV) and Fe2O3 (530.6 and 531.7 eV) [53]. In summary, GA-SiDs effectively adhere to the surface of Q235 steel through electrostatic interactions and/or coordination interactions involving the Fe atoms from the Q235 substrates and N, O atoms from the surface of silicon quantum dots.

Generally, the coordination interactions and adsorption of quantum dot into iron surfaces are widely recognized as significant mechanisms for corrosion inhibition [54]. Unmodified SiDs inhibit corrosion via physical adsorption, forming a hydrophobic layer that blocks corrosive ions [55]. Their inherent surface defects and residual Si–H/Si–O groups enable weak chemisorption with Fe, though less effectively than GA-SiDs due to poorer solubility and fewer active sites [56]. The current findings provide support for the effective inhibition performance of GA-SiDs for Q235 steel corrosion in an acid solution. Figure S3 shows a schematic view of the anticorrosion mechanism of GA-SiDs. The inhibitory effect of GA-SiDs primarily relies on chemisorption via metal coordination-chelation interaction and the formation of a hydrophobic protective film as a physical barrier. Specifically, GA-SiDs are characterized by a diverse array of functional groups compared with SiDs, such as –OH, and –COOH, rendering them prone to chemical adsorption onto iron surfaces and reinforcing the establishment of coordinate bonds between Fe atoms and O atoms. Additionally, silicon quantum dots are nanomaterials exhibiting hydrophobic properties due to their high surface energy, resulting in their sub-aggregation. The characteristic allows for the agglomeration and settling of particles on the iron surface in the corrosive solution, leading to the formation of a hydrophobic layer. The existence of surface defects in silicon quantum dots promotes the accumulation of charges and physical adsorption with iron. The adsorption of GA-SiDs on the metal surface can lead to further agglomeration with those present in the corrosive solution, thereby increasing the driving force for a more compact and hydrophobic film formation on the metal surface. This phenomenon contributes to an enhanced protective effect against corrosion. Consequently, the presence of hydrophobic film hinders the aggressive adsorption of hydrogen ions and chloride ions onto the steel surface, resulting in a substantial decrease in the formation of corrosion products. In conclusion, the combination of chemisorption and agglomeration of GA-SiDs plays a crucial role in the formation and maintenance of a hydrophobic film that hinders corrosion processes on Q235 steel surfaces.

In this work, SiDs corrosion inhibitors with a fluorescent effect were synthesized using the hydrothermal method. Additionally, GA was introduced to prepare the enhanced water solubility GA-SiDs. A comparative analysis was conducted on their characterization and corrosion inhibition performance. HRTEM, XPS, and fluorescence properties analysis indicated the successful preparation of silicon quantum dots. FTIR confirmed the abundance of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of GA-SiDs, while DLS measurements demonstrated enhancements in water solubility following surface engineering. The analysis of weight loss and adsorption behavior revealed that SiDs and GA-SiDs are capable of effectively adsorbing onto and protecting Q235 steel in a 0.5 M HCl solution. EIS studies confirmed that GA-SiDs consistently demonstrate a higher level of inhibition efficiency at concentrations ranging from 20 to 100 mg/L compared to SiDs during the immersion period of 24 h. PDP tests revealed that SiDs and GA-SiDs can be categorized as mixed-type corrosion inhibitor. SEM/EDS, WCA, 3D-LSM, AFM, TOF-SIMS, and XPS suggested the hydrophobic protective layer consisted of the agglomerations of SiDs and GA-SiDs. Above all, the GA-SiDs with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups exhibited enhanced corrosion inhibition performance. The inhibitory effect primarily relies on the formation of a hydrophobic protective layer as a physical barrier and the chemisorption via metal coordination-chelation interaction. Combining the inherent characteristics of silicon elements, such as excellent biocompatibility, environmental friendliness, and low cost, this work provides a significant guideline for the development and performance prediction of novel environmentally friendly quantum dots corrosion inhibitors in both theoretical and practical aspects.
